planting.note: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface in fall to allow winter cold stratification to break dormancy; do not transplant, as seedlings immediately develop a deep taproot that cannot be disturbed.

directions: Direct sow on the soil surface in fall and allow natural cold stratification over winter to break seed dormancy; germination occurs the following spring. Compass plant is one of the most iconic tallgrass prairie species — its deeply pinnately lobed basal leaves reliably orient their flat faces north and south, giving early settlers a natural compass on the treeless plains. The plant grows slowly for the first several years, investing energy almost entirely in a massive taproot that can reach 10–15 feet deep, which makes it extraordinarily drought tolerant and effectively immortal once established, with individual plants potentially living over a century. Bright yellow daisy-like flowers 2–3 inches across appear on tall branching stems from July through September, providing pollen and nectar for native bees and seeds for birds.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow winter cold stratification; seeds require exposure to cold and moisture to break dormancy and will germinate in spring without any additional treatment.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface in fall to take advantage of natural cold stratification, and expect germination the following spring after a period of consistent cold. Prairie dock is one of the most visually dramatic native prairie plants, producing enormous rough-textured basal leaves up to 18 inches long that resemble the ears of an elephant — these bold leaves persist all season and provide striking foliage interest even when the plant is not in bloom. In midsummer, nearly leafless flowering stems shoot up to 5–8 feet tall and are topped with loose clusters of small yellow daisy flowers that attract a wide range of native pollinators through late summer. Like all Silphium, prairie dock is extremely slow to mature from seed but extraordinarily long-lived, forming a deep resinous taproot that anchors it through drought and fire.

planting.note: Direct sow in early fall so seeds receive natural cold stratification over winter; this is the most reliable method — spring-sown seed should be cold-stratified in the refrigerator for 60–90 days before planting.

directions: Direct sow on the soil surface in fall and allow winter cold to break seed dormancy naturally, with germination occurring in spring. Rosinweed is the most adaptable and garden-friendly of the Silphiums, distinguished by its entire (non-lobed), opposite, slightly rough-textured leaves and bushy branching habit that makes it shorter and more compact than its prairie relatives. Bright yellow daisy-like flowers with prominent yellow centers bloom prolifically from July through September, providing an outstanding nectar source for native bees including bumblebees and long-tongued specialists. Over many years rosinweed forms a large, robust clump supported by a deep resinous taproot — the genus name Silphium refers to the sticky resinous sap that oozes from cut stems, which Native Americans used medicinally.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination, or sow in early spring directly on the soil surface as seeds need light to germinate; press seeds gently into the surface but do not cover.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface in fall or early spring, pressing them lightly into contact with soil but not covering, as they require light for germination. Grayhead coneflower is one of the most graceful native prairie composites, with long drooping bright yellow petals reflexed sharply back from a distinctive tall oval gray-brown cone that releases an anise-like fragrance when rubbed — this upright cone distinguishes it clearly from the flatter cones of Rudbeckia. Plants bloom prolifically from July through September on branching 3–5 foot stems clothed in pinnately divided leaves, and they self-seed freely to naturalize into expanding colonies in prairie restorations and meadow gardens. Extremely drought and heat tolerant once established, grayhead coneflower thrives in poor, well-drained soils and is an important late-summer nectar source for bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface in fall or early spring — do not cover seeds as light aids germination; fall sowing with natural cold stratification produces the most vigorous spring seedlings and often results in first-year bloom.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface in fall for natural cold stratification and reliable spring germination, or start in early spring — plants often bloom their first year from seed, making this one of the fastest-blooming native prairie perennials. Prairie coneflower is instantly recognizable by its tall thimble-shaped or columnar central cone, which can be two to three times taller than it is wide, surrounded by drooping ray petals that are typically bright yellow but in some populations are bicolor yellow and red at the base, resembling a tiny Mexican sombrero and giving rise to its folk name. Plants bloom continuously from June through September on wiry stems with finely divided blue-green pinnate foliage, and they thrive in lean, dry, well-drained soils including sandy plains, gravel roadsides, and xeriscape gardens where other perennials fail. Self-seeding colonies spread steadily, providing long-season nectar for native bees and seed for birds into fall.
